title: System Setup and Verification
description: Set up the experimental environment, verify hardware capabilities, and install necessary tools for energy monitoring and scheduler development.

steps.step1: Verify Linux kernel version >= 6.12 by running 'uname -r'. If older, sched_ext may not be available.
steps.step2: Check for RAPL energy monitoring support: 'ls /sys/class/powercap/intel-rapl' or 'ls /sys/class/powercap/'. Document available energy domains (package, core, dram).
steps.step3: Verify sched_ext availability: check '/sys/kernel/sched_ext' exists and schedulers can be loaded via bpftool.
steps.step4: Install required packages: clang, llvm, libbpf-dev, linux-headers-$(uname -r), bpftool, perf, sysstat, jq.
steps.step5: Clone sched_ext scheduler repository (https://github.com/sched-ext/scx) for reference implementations and build system.
steps.step6: Check CPU topology: 'lscpu -e' to identify core types (P-cores/E-cores on Intel or big.LITTLE on ARM). Document heterogeneous capabilities.
steps.step7: Verify hardware performance counter (PMC) availability: 'perf list' should show cycles, instructions, cache-misses, etc.

title: Workload Benchmark Preparation
description: Prepare and validate benchmark workloads representing different workload classes (CPU-bound, memory-bound, mixed, I/O-bound).

steps.step1: Install sysbench: 'apt-get install sysbench' or build from source. This provides CPU and memory benchmarks.
steps.step2: Create CPU-bound synthetic workload: compile a program that performs intensive computation (e.g., matrix multiplication, prime calculation). Source code in benchmarks/cpu_bound.c.
steps.step3: Create memory-bound synthetic workload: compile a program with random memory access patterns causing high cache miss rates. Source code in benchmarks/memory_bound.c.
steps.step4: Create mixed workload: combine CPU and memory operations with configurable ratio (50/50 default). Source code in benchmarks/mixed.c.
steps.step5: Create I/O-bound workload: simple program reading/writing files in bursts with sleep periods. Source code in benchmarks/io_bound.c.
steps.step6: Install nginx and wrk for web server benchmarking: 'apt-get install nginx' and build wrk from source.
steps.step7: Download and build llama.cpp (lightweight CPU-based LLM inference) from https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp for real-world mixed workload.
steps.step8: Download a small transformer model (e.g., TinyLlama-1.1B Q4_0 quantized, ~600MB) for inference testing.
steps.step9: Validate all workloads run correctly without scheduler modifications and document baseline execution characteristics.
steps.step10: Create workload configuration files defining duration, intensity, and resource demands for each benchmark.

title: Linux EEVDF Baseline Collection
description: Collect comprehensive baseline measurements using the default Linux EEVDF scheduler across all workload types.

steps.step1: Ensure default EEVDF scheduler is active: 'cat /sys/kernel/sched_ext/current' should show 'ext' not loaded or system using default.
steps.step2: For each workload type (CPU-bound, memory-bound, mixed, I/O-bound), run with 3 random seeds: 42, 123, 999.
steps.step3: Single workload execution: Run each workload type in isolation for 60 seconds. Record: total energy (Joules via RAPL), execution time, instructions retired, cache misses.
steps.step4: Mixed workload execution: Run pairs of workloads concurrently (CPU+memory, CPU+mixed, memory+mixed) for 60 seconds each. Record total system energy and per-process metrics.
steps.step5: Multi-process scalability: Run 4, 8, 16, 32 concurrent instances of CPU-bound and memory-bound workloads for 60 seconds each.
steps.step6: Web server workload: Start nginx, run wrk with 100 connections, 4 threads for 60 seconds. Measure throughput (req/sec), latency (p50, p99), and total energy.
steps.step7: LLM inference workload: Run llama.cpp with batch sizes 1, 4, 8 for 60 seconds each. Measure tokens/sec and energy per token.
steps.step8: For all runs, use perf stat to collect: cycles, instructions, cache-references, cache-misses, LLC-load-misses.
steps.step9: Save all results to results/baseline/raw/ with naming convention: eevdf_{workload}_{seed}_{timestamp}.json
steps.step10: Calculate and document baseline statistics: mean energy, std dev, energy per instruction, performance variability.

title: Simple Energy-Aware Baseline (Wattmeter-style)
description: Implement and evaluate a simple energy-fair scheduler (equal energy per process) as a second baseline for comparison.

steps.step1: Implement simple_energy_scheduler.c using sched_ext: tracks per-process energy via RAPL, attempts to equalize energy consumption across processes.
steps.step2: Compile scheduler: 'clang -O2 -target bpf -c simple_energy_scheduler.c -o simple_energy_scheduler.o' and load with 'bpftool struct_ops load'.
steps.step3: Verify scheduler loads correctly and sched_ext watchdog does not trigger.
steps.step4: Run the same experiment suite as EEVDF baseline (single workloads, mixed pairs, scalability, web server, LLM) with 3 seeds.
steps.step5: Record metrics: total energy, execution time, fairness (energy variance across processes), scheduling latency.
steps.step7: Compare simple energy-fair scheduler vs EEVDF: document where simple energy awareness helps or hurts performance.

title: WattSched Full Implementation
description: Implement the complete WattSched scheduler with all components: energy monitoring, workload classification, and topology-aware scheduling.

steps.step1: Implement energy_monitor.c BPF program: Uses perf_event_open to read RAPL energy counters on context switch, stores per-process energy in BPF map 'pid_to_energy'.
steps.step2: Implement workload_classifier.c BPF program: Reads PMCs (cycles, instructions, cache-misses), calculates IPC and cache miss rate, classifies into CPU-bound (IPC>1.5, miss<5%), memory-bound (IPC<0.5, miss>20%), mixed, or I/O-bound (sleep time > 50%). Uses EWMA with alpha=0.3 for smoothing.
steps.step3: Implement scheduler_core.c: sched_ext callbacks (enqueue, dispatch, running, stopping), maintains per-CPU runqueues, implements topology-aware placement.
steps.step4: Implement topology policy: On heterogeneous systems, CPU-bound tasks prefer P-cores/big cores, memory-bound tasks prefer E-cores/little cores. Use CPU affinity hints via sched_ext.
steps.step5: Implement adaptive time slicing: CPU-bound get 8ms slices, memory-bound get 3ms slices, I/O-bound get 1ms slices. Adjust based on energy efficiency tracking.
steps.step6: Integrate all components into wattsched.bpf.c and userspace loader wattsched.c for map initialization and scheduler loading.
steps.step7: Compile with: 'make wattsched' using standard sched_ext build infrastructure.
steps.step8: Load scheduler and verify no compilation errors or verifier rejections.
steps.step9: Run smoke test: Start scheduler, run 4 CPU-bound and 4 memory-bound tasks for 30 seconds, verify stable operation without watchdog triggers.
steps.step10: Debug and fix any BPF verifier issues or runtime errors. Document any simplifications made.

title: WattSched Performance Evaluation
description: Evaluate WattSched against baselines across all workload scenarios.

steps.step1: Load WattSched scheduler via 'bpftool struct_ops load wattsched.bpf.o /sys/kernel/sched_ext'.
steps.step2: Verify scheduler is active: 'cat /sys/kernel/sched_ext/current' should show 'wattsched'.
steps.step3: Run single workload experiments (CPU, memory, mixed, I/O) for 60 seconds each with seeds 42, 123, 999. Record total energy, execution time, classification accuracy.
steps.step4: Run mixed workload pairs (CPU+memory, CPU+mixed, memory+mixed, CPU+CPU, memory+memory) for 60 seconds each with 3 seeds. Record energy savings vs baselines.
steps.step5: Run scalability test: 4, 8, 16, 32 concurrent processes of each type for 60 seconds. Measure energy efficiency and scheduling fairness.
steps.step6: Run web server benchmark: nginx + wrk for 60 seconds. Compare throughput, latency, and energy vs baselines.
steps.step7: Run LLM inference: llama.cpp with batch sizes 1, 4, 8 for 60 seconds each. Measure tokens/sec and energy per token.
steps.step8: For each run, capture classification decisions: log transitions between workload classes and migration decisions.
steps.step10: Unload scheduler between runs to ensure clean state: 'echo disable > /sys/kernel/sched_ext/control'

title: Workload Classification Ablation
description: Remove workload classification to isolate its contribution to energy savings.

steps.step1: Create wattsched_no_classify.c: Same as WattSched but with classification disabled - treats all workloads as 'mixed' type.
steps.step2: Compile and load the ablated scheduler.
steps.step3: Run subset of key experiments: mixed CPU+memory pairs, scalability (8 processes), and web server workload.
steps.step5: Record energy consumption, execution time, and compare to full WattSched.
steps.step8: Document whether classification enables heterogeneous core assignment benefits.

title: Topology Optimization Ablation
description: Remove heterogeneous core assignment to measure its specific contribution.

steps.step1: Create wattsched_no_topology.c: Same classification but no core-type preference - schedule on any available core.
steps.step2: Only meaningful on heterogeneous systems. Skip if system is homogeneous (all cores identical).
steps.step3: Compile and load the ablated scheduler.
steps.step4: Run same subset as classification ablation: mixed pairs, scalability, web server with 3 seeds.
steps.step6: Calculate topology optimization contribution vs full WattSched.
steps.step8: Analyze whether memory-bound tasks on little cores actually save energy as hypothesized.

title: Adaptive Time Slicing Ablation
description: Test with uniform time slices instead of workload-adaptive slicing.

steps.step1: Create wattsched_uniform_slice.c: All workload types get 4ms time slices (uniform average of adaptive slices).
steps.step2: Compile and load the ablated scheduler.
steps.step3: Run reduced experiment set: mixed CPU+memory pairs with 3 seeds, 60 seconds each.
steps.step4: Focus on context switch rate and scheduling overhead metrics.
steps.step5: Compare energy and performance vs full WattSched.
steps.step7: Document impact of adaptive slicing on context switch overhead and cache efficiency.

title: Overhead Analysis
description: Measure the runtime overhead of energy monitoring, classification, and BPF execution.

steps.step1: Implement microbenchmark: Empty sched_ext scheduler (scx_simple) that does minimal work - serves as 'no overhead' baseline.
steps.step2: Measure scheduling latency: Use ftrace or bpftrace to record time from enqueue to running callback.
steps.step3: Compare latencies: EEVDF (in-kernel CFS) vs scx_simple vs WattSched.
steps.step4: Measure BPF execution time: Use bpftrace to time BPF program execution on context switches.
steps.step5: Measure PMC read overhead: Compare running with/without perf_event counter reads.
steps.step6: Run CPU-bound workload (max scheduling frequency) and record context switches per second.
steps.step8: Verify overhead remains under 2% as per success criteria.

title: Statistical Analysis and Success Criteria
description: Analyze all experimental results, compute statistical significance, and verify success criteria.

steps.step1: Aggregate all raw results from results/*/raw/ into structured data format (JSON/CSV).
steps.step2: Calculate mean and standard deviation for energy consumption across 3 seeds for each configuration.
steps.step3: Compute energy reduction percentage: (EEVDF_energy - WattSched_energy) / EEVDF_energy * 100% for each workload.
steps.step5: Perform paired t-test between EEVDF and WattSched results to verify statistical significance (p < 0.05).
steps.step6: Check success criteria: (1) ≥15% energy reduction on mixed workloads, (2) <5% performance degradation, (3) <2% overhead, (4) no instability.
steps.step7: Check refutation criteria: If energy reduction <10%, performance degradation >10%, overhead >5%, or instability occurs.
steps.step8: Generate summary table: scheduler vs workload vs energy vs performance vs p-value.
steps.step9: Save processed results to results/processed/summary_stats.json and results/processed/success_criteria.json
steps.step10: Write conclusion: Which hypotheses are confirmed, which are refuted, and why.

title: Figure Generation for Paper
description: Generate publication-quality figures showing energy savings, performance tradeoffs, and ablation results.

steps.step1: Create Figure 1 - Energy Comparison Bar Chart: X-axis = workload type, Y-axis = energy (Joules), grouped bars for EEVDF, Simple Energy, WattSched. Include error bars for 3 seeds.
steps.step2: Create Figure 2 - Performance-Energy Tradeoff: Scatter plot with X=execution time (normalized), Y=energy (normalized), points for each scheduler. Lower-left is better.
steps.step3: Create Figure 3 - Ablation Study: Bar chart showing energy consumption for full WattSched vs no-classification vs no-topology vs uniform-slice. Percentage labels showing contribution.
steps.step4: Create Figure 4 - Scalability Analysis: X-axis = number of processes, Y-axis = energy per instruction (efficiency), lines for each scheduler.
steps.step5: Create Figure 5 - Workload Classification Accuracy: Confusion matrix showing predicted vs actual workload types on synthetic benchmarks.
steps.step6: Create Figure 6 - Overhead Breakdown: Stacked bar showing time spent in BPF execution, PMC reading, classification, and scheduling decisions.
steps.step7: Use matplotlib or gnuplot for figure generation. Save as PDF and PNG (300 DPI minimum).
steps.step8: Save figures to results/visualizations/ with descriptive filenames.
steps.step9: Create summary table for paper: Scheduler comparison with energy, performance, overhead metrics.
steps.step10: Verify all figures have proper labels, legends, and captions suitable for academic paper.
